children.name: Phaethon
children.description: Slender, predominantly white seabirds with extremely long, streamer-like central tail feathers that give them an elegant, tern-like appearance. Tropicbirds are pelagic, ranging across tropical and subtropical oceans, and are famed for their spectacular aerial courtship displays where pairs fly backwards and cross their tail streamers. They are named after Phaëthon, son of the sun god Helios.

children.name: Phaethon aethereus
children.description: The largest tropicbird, with a distinctive bright red bill and long white tail streamers. It breeds on rocky islands and cliffs in the tropical Atlantic, eastern Pacific, and Indian Oceans, including the Galápagos and Caribbean. It feeds by plunge-diving for fish and squid, often from considerable heights.

children.name: Phaethon rubricauda
children.description: Easily identified by its vivid coral-red bill and bright red tail streamers, the reddest of any seabird. It breeds on tropical islands across the Indian and Pacific Oceans, from the coast of East Africa to the Hawaiian Islands and Easter Island. Its courtship flight involves multiple birds flying in circles high above the colony, crossing and recrossing their tail streamers.

children.name: Phaethon lepturus
children.description: The smallest and most abundant tropicbird, with a yellow-orange bill and white tail streamers. It has a pantropical distribution, breeding on islands across the Atlantic, Indian and Pacific Oceans. It is a graceful flier and powerful plunge-diver, often seen soaring high above its nesting islands.
